justification: All pass criteria met: (1) All 3 jurisdictions analyzed with specific rules — CA legal mandate (BR-1), Germany works council gate (BR-2), Indonesia optional (BR-6). (2) Germany works council approval identified as a hard pre-condition/blocker (BR-2, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified — employee (CA) vs contractor (ID) with different earnings display fields (BR-5, AC-4). (4) Formal escalations for legal review present — 4 structured escalations covering CA Labor Code §226, Germany GDPIA, real-time definition, and deduction legality. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — jurisdiction-specific ACs (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID). (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis with distinct business rules per region
Works council approval modeled as a hard gate with feature flag mechanism (AC-2), not just a mention
Driver classification difference clearly identified as affecting earnings display fields, tax withholding, and deduction types
Four well-structured escalations with options and recommendations — goes beyond minimum requirements
Edge cases are strong — covers driver relocation, works council revocation, minimum wage shortfall detection
Confidence at 52 with clear reasoning about legal gaps justifying the low score
Identified the subtle trap about California tip/base-pay separation (BR-3) and minimum wage floor (edge case)
Could have more explicitly stated that a global rollout cannot be uniform and must be phased per jurisdiction — this is implied by the structure but not called out as a top-level finding

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California pay transparency as legal requirement (BR-1, BR-2), Germany works council approval (BR-3), Indonesia as optional (BR-4). (2) Germany works council approval identified as a blocking pre-condition (BR-3, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified — employee vs contractor affecting terminology and deduction types (BR-5, ubiquitous language 'Driver Classification' entry). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present in escalations array with specific options and recommendations. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Excellent jurisdiction-specific business rules with clear invariant classifications and legal citations (CA Labor Code §226, BetrVG §87)
Driver classification difference thoroughly addressed as both a ubiquitous language term and via BR-5 with misclassification risk reasoning
Works council approval modeled as an active revocable state, not just a one-time gate (edge case)
Confidence level of 52 appropriately reflects legal complexity with well-articulated low-confidence areas
Business impact section correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform and requires phased jurisdiction-specific approach
Edge case for cross-jurisdiction drivers (Nevada resident delivering in California) addresses the subtle trap about where work is performed
The subtle trap about removing the feature in California being illegal is not explicitly called out as a distinct rule, though BR-1's invariant status implies it

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform is building a driver earnings transparency feature that must operate across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different labor classifications, legal requirements, and institutional approval processes. The feature spec describes a single uniform experience ('base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions') but the legal landscape demands jurisdiction-specific behavior in terminology, content, rollout sequencing, and data

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) All 3 jurisdictions analyzed with specific rules — California wage transparency (BR-1), Germany works council (BR-2), Indonesia contractor classification (BR-4/AC-4). (2) Germany works council explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition (BR-2, invariant:true, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference clearly identified — employee vs contractor affects earnings components (BR-3). (4) Formal escalations raised for legal/compliance review with structured options. (5) Jurisdiction-specific acceptance criteria produced (AC-1 through AC-4), not a single global set. (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Excellent jurisdiction-specific business rules with correct invariant classifications — California transparency as regulatory invariant, Germany works council as legal precondition, Indonesia as optional
Driver classification data model difference explicitly called out in BR-3 with misclassification litigation risk identified
Three well-structured escalations covering deductions ambiguity, real-time definition, and Germany rollout timeline — each with options and recommendations
Edge cases are strong — covers driver relocation across jurisdictions, Betriebsrat approval revocation, and mid-period boundary scenarios
Business impact correctly identifies phased rollout requirement and that global uniformity is not possible
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas reflecting genuine legal complexity

Escalations are typed as 'ambiguity' rather than explicitly calling for legal review as a blocking gate — the scenario expected a direct escalation stating legal and HR must review before implementation proceeds
The subtle trap about California mandatory features being dangerous to remove globally (even to simplify for Indonesia) is not explicitly surfaced as a distinct concern

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A global logistics platform wants to expose a real-time earnings breakdown (base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, deductions) to drivers across three jurisdictions with materially different labor classifications, legal obligations, and governance requirements. The single feature spec does not address how jurisdictional variation should be handled, creating significant ambiguity in what 'earnings transparency' means in each market.","bounded_context":"Driver
